title: To provide a per diem allowance for Members of Congress for the costs of lodging, meals, and incidental expenses incurred because of travel to and from the Washington Metropolitan Area in order to cast votes in Congress, and for other purposes.
plain_summary: This bill would give members of Congress money to help pay for hotels, food, and travel costs when they come to Washington DC to vote. Only members who live outside the Washington area would get this money. They would only get paid for days when they actually show up and vote in person. The goal is to help cover the expenses that members face when they travel to do their job in Congress.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on House Administration.

sections.title: Section 1  Per Diem Allowances for Members Incurring Costs in Travel to and from Washington Metropolitan Area in Order to Cast Votes in Congress
sections.text: This section creates a daily allowance system to help Members of Congress pay for travel costs when they come to Washington DC to vote. A Member of Congress who travels from their home to the Washington area to cast votes can receive money to help pay for two types of expenses. First, they can get money to help pay for a place to stay while in the Washington area. Second, they can get money to help pay for meals and other small expenses while in the Washington area. However, there are some important exceptions to this rule. Members who already live in the Washington area cannot receive this daily allowance because they do not need to travel. Also, Members cannot receive this allowance if they are already getting money for these same expenses from other allowances they receive as part of their job. For House members, this other allowance is called the Members Representational Allowance. For Senators, it is called the Senators Official Personnel and Office Expense Account. The rules for getting the lodging allowance are specific. A Member can only receive money for a place to stay on days when their part of Congress holds votes and the Member actually shows up in person to vote on every vote that day. The rules for getting meal and expense money are also specific. Members can get this money on days when they receive the lodging allowance. They can also get meal money for the day before a voting day if they travel to Washington that day, and for the day after a voting day if they travel home that day. The amount of money Members can receive will be the same amount that federal employees would get for similar travel expenses under existing government rules set by the Administrator of General Services. Two congressional committees will create the detailed rules for how this system works. The House Administration Committee will make rules for House members and delegates. The Senate Rules and Administration Committee will make rules for Senators. These committees must try to make sure that this daily allowance money is not counted as taxable income for the Members. The new rules these committees create will replace any existing travel expense rules they currently have. This section defines some important terms. A designated residence means the home address a Member officially chooses in their home district or state. For House members, this must be in the district they represent or at least in the same state. For Senators, this must be in the state they represent. The term Member of the House of Representatives includes delegates and the resident commissioner who represent territories. The Washington Metropolitan Area includes Washington DC, certain cities and counties in Virginia including Alexandria, Falls Church, Fairfax, Arlington and Fairfax County, and Montgomery and Prince Georges counties in Maryland. This new system will start with the current One Hundred Nineteenth Congress and continue for all future Congresses.
